candidate.committeeName: COMMITTEE TO ELECT CHIP NEIMAN TO HOUSE DISTRICT 1

responses:
641 State Highway 24  Devils Tower, WY
Lived in this area since 1974
Born-Belle Fourche, SD

My qualifications come from a lifetime of experience in agriculture, business, conservation, and public service. Most importantly, I understand the people and communities of northeast Wyoming because I have lived and worked alongside them my entire life. I am committed to continuing to be a steady conservative voice who puts Wyoming values first and delivers results for the citizens of District 1.

Wyoming's biggest challenge is preserving our quality of life while creating opportunities for future generations. We must grow and diversify our economy without abandoning the industries that built our state—agriculture, energy, mining, and tourism. Rising property taxes, workforce shortages, healthcare access, and federal overreach remain significant concerns.

Our greatest opportunity is Wyoming's people. With abundant natural resources, strong communities, and a business-friendly environment, we can attract investment and create good-paying jobs. We should support responsible energy development, expand value-added agriculture, reduce unnecessary regulations, and invest in workforce training.

As a legislator, I focus on results over rhetoric. I support protecting private property rights, strengthening Wyoming's savings, promoting economic growth, and preserving our agricultural heritage. My goal is to ensure Wyoming remains a place where families and businesses can thrive.

Affordable, accessible healthcare is essential to Wyoming families, especially in rural communities. While the Legislature cannot solve every healthcare challenge, we can remove barriers, increase competition, and support local providers.

I support strengthening rural hospitals and clinics, expanding telehealth, and addressing workforce shortages by recruiting and retaining healthcare professionals. We should also reduce unnecessary regulations that increase costs and limit access to care.

Wyoming should pursue market-based solutions that give patients more choices, greater price transparency, and improved access to care. Mental health services and preventive care are also important priorities.

As a legislator, I have supported efforts to improve healthcare access while maintaining fiscal responsibility. My goal is to ensure Wyoming residents can obtain quality healthcare close to home at a cost they can afford while preserving vital local providers.

Wyoming's public lands are essential to our economy, outdoor heritage, and way of life. I do not support wholesale sales or transfers of federal public lands that would reduce public access, threaten multiple-use management, or burden Wyoming taxpayers.

I support giving Wyoming a stronger voice in federal land management decisions. Too often, policies are developed in Washington without sufficient input from the people most affected. Local governments, landowners, permittees, sportsmen, tribes, and state agencies should have meaningful involvement in decisions affecting our communities.

I support active management of public lands, including responsible grazing, energy development, recreation, wildlife habitat improvement, and wildfire mitigation. Healthy forests and rangelands require stewardship, not neglect.

My goal is to keep Wyoming's public lands accessible, productive, and sustainable while protecting jobs, supporting local communities, and preserving opport

I support targeted property tax relief, particularly for long-term homeowners, seniors, veterans, and Wyoming residents on fixed incomes who are being squeezed by rising property valuations. At the same time, we must ensure local governments can maintain roads, public safety, emergency services, and other essential functions.

The Legislature should provide local flexibility and avoid shifting costs to future generations. We should also continue growing Wyoming's economy and broadening the tax base through responsible business development, energy production, tourism, and value-added agriculture.

My approach is to find practical solutions that provide meaningful tax relief while preserving essential local services. As Vice Chairman of the Senate Appropriations Committee, I have worked to strengthen Wyoming's savings and maintain fiscal stability, and I believe property tax policy should reflect that same balance.

The Legislature has an important role in economic development, but government does not create prosperity—people, businesses, and entrepreneurs do. Our job is to create an environment where private investment can succeed through low taxes, reasonable regulations, reliable infrastructure, and a skilled workforce.

Wyoming's economy is built on agriculture, energy, mining, and tourism. We should support those industries while encouraging opportunities in manufacturing and value-added agriculture. Economic development should focus on creating jobs, expanding opportunities, and broadening the tax base.

I have consistently supported policies that make Wyoming more competitive, including regulatory reform, workforce development, infrastructure investment, and responsible resource development. The Legislature's role is to remove barriers to success and provide a stable business climate so Wyoming businesses, communities, and families can prosper.

I believe campaign contributions are a form of free speech and an important way for citizens to participate in the political process. Voters deserve to know who is supporting candidates and how campaign funds are spent.

I have always supported transparency and timely disclosure of campaign contributions and expenditures. Wyoming's system generally works well because campaign reports are publicly available.

I am open to reforms that improve transparency and make campaign finance information easier to access and understand. One principle is non-negotiable: there should never be political contributions solicited, accepted, or exchanged in leadership offices, legislative chambers, or the halls surrounding those chambers.

My record demonstrates that I make decisions based on what is best for Wyoming and the people of Senate District 1, not campaign contributions. Public service requires independence, integrity, accountability, and public trust.

candidatesInDistrict.committeeName: COMMITTEE TO ELECT CHIP NEIMAN TO HOUSE DISTRICT 1

questions: Where do you live currently? How long have you lived there? How long have you lived in Wyoming? Where were you born?
What age will you be on Election Day, Nov. 3, 2026?
Please tell the voters about yourself including your background and qualifications.
What are the biggest challenges and opportunities facing Wyoming today? As a legislator, how would you address them?

We asked WyoFile readers to rank issues that are important to them, and healthcare costs and access topped the list. What can the Legislature do to make healthcare more affordable and accessible to Wyomingites?
Do you support efforts to sell or transfer public land in Wyoming? How do you plan to ensure that state and federal land is managed in the best interest of the people of Wyoming?
No two Wyoming counties are the same when it comes to how they generate revenue. As a legislator, how would you approach the public's desire for property tax relief with local revenue needs?
What role, if any, should the Wyoming Legislature play in economic development?
Explain your values when it comes to campaign donations. Does Wyoming's system for monitoring campaign donations and expenditures need reform?
pageProps.questionnaireIntro:
The answers below were solicited from candidates via a written questionnaire created by WyoFile reporters and editors in June 2026. Responses are presented exactly as submitted, without fact-checking, wordsmithing or editing for grammar, punctuation or spelling errors. 

Legislative candidates were invited to respond to the questionnaire several times by email and by phone. Out of fairness to the candidates who met the deadline, WyoFile will not add responses after the guide's publication.
pageProps.aboutProject:
WyoFile produced this guide with coding and web development by Thomas Musselman, editing by Tennessee Watson and production assistance by Calla Shosh. Contact Tennessee Watson with questions, corrections or suggestions at <tennessee@wyofile.com>.

WyoFile is a Wyoming 501(c)3 nonprofit, independent, member-supported, public-interest news service reporting on the people, places and policy of Wyoming. WyoFile's [donor base](https://wyofile.com/supporters/) is politically diverse, including some donors who are candidates in this year's election. We maintain separation between sources of revenue and editorial direction, decisions and oversight. Acceptance of financial support does not constitute implied or actual endorsement of donors or their products, services or opinions, and does not confer editorial influence, nor does donor support imply any endorsement of WyoFile's reporting, commentary or editorial position.

This material is available for republication by select media outlets. Contact Collaborations Editor Rebecca Huntington at <rebecca@wyofile.com> to learn how.
